question:
Let $k$ be a positive integer. Prove that there exist polynomials
$P_0(n), P_1(n), \dots, P_{k-1}(n)$ (which may depend on $k$) such that
for any integer $n$,
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{n}{k} \right\rfloor^k = P_0(n) + P_1(n) \left\lfloor
\frac{n}{k} \right\rfloor + \cdots + P_{k-1}(n) \left\lfloor \frac{n}{k}
\right\rfloor^{k-1}.
\]
($\lfloor a \rfloor$ means the largest integer $\leq a$.)
solution:
For $n$ an integer, we have
$\left\lfloor \frac{n}{k} \right\rfloor = \frac{n-j}{k}$ for $j$
the unique integer in $\{0,\dots,k-1\}$ congruent to $n$ modulo $k$;
hence
\[
\prod_{j=0}^{k-1} \left( \left\lfloor \frac{n}{k} \right\rfloor - \frac{n-j}{k}
\right) = 0.
\]
By expanding this out, we obtain
the desired polynomials $P_0(n), \dots, P_{k-1}(n)$.

\textbf{Remark:} Variants of this solution are possible that
construct the $P_i$
less explicitly, using Lagrange interpolation or Vandermonde determinants.

descriptive_long.question:
Problem:
<<<
Let $divisor$ be a positive integer. Prove that there exist polynomials
$polyzero(integer), polyone(integer), \dots, polylast(integer)$ (which may depend on $divisor$) such that
for any integer $integer$,
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{integer}{divisor} \right\rfloor^{divisor} = polyzero(integer) + polyone(integer) \left\lfloor
\frac{integer}{divisor} \right\rfloor + \cdots + polylast(integer) \left\lfloor \frac{integer}{divisor}
\right\rfloor^{divisor-1}.
\]
($\lfloor a \rfloor$ means the largest integer $\leq a$.)
>>>
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution:
<<<
For $integer$ an integer, we have
$\left\lfloor \frac{integer}{divisor} \right\rfloor = \frac{integer-residue}{divisor}$ for $residue$
the unique integer in $\{0,\dots,divisor-1\}$ congruent to $integer$ modulo $divisor$;
hence
\[
\prod_{residue=0}^{divisor-1} \left( \left\lfloor \frac{integer}{divisor} \right\rfloor - \frac{integer-residue}{divisor}
\right) = 0.
\]
By expanding this out, we obtain
the desired polynomials $polyzero(integer), \dots, polylast(integer)$.

\textbf{Remark:} Variants of this solution are possible that
construct the $polygen$
less explicitly, using Lagrange interpolation or Vandermonde determinants.
>>>

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $dandelion$ be a positive integer. Prove that there exist polynomials
$rainstorm(sapphire), buttercup(sapphire), \dots, sunflower(sapphire)$ (which may depend on $dandelion$) such that
for any integer $sapphire$,
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{sapphire}{dandelion} \right\rfloor^{dandelion} = rainstorm(sapphire) + buttercup(sapphire) \left\lfloor
\frac{sapphire}{dandelion} \right\rfloor + \cdots + sunflower(sapphire) \left\lfloor \frac{sapphire}{dandelion}
\right\rfloor^{dandelion-1}.
\]
($\lfloor a \rfloor$ means the largest integer $\leq a$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
For $sapphire$ an integer, we have
$\left\lfloor \frac{sapphire}{dandelion} \right\rfloor = \frac{sapphire-marigold}{dandelion}$ for $marigold$
the unique integer in $\{0,\dots,dandelion-1\}$ congruent to $sapphire$ modulo $dandelion$;
hence
\[
\prod_{marigold=0}^{dandelion-1} \left( \left\lfloor \frac{sapphire}{dandelion} \right\rfloor - \frac{sapphire-marigold}{dandelion}
\right) = 0.
\]
By expanding this out, we obtain
the desired polynomials $rainstorm(sapphire), \dots, sunflower(sapphire)$.

\textbf{Remark:} Variants of this solution are possible that
construct the $daffodil$
less explicitly, using Lagrange interpolation or Vandermonde determinants.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $endlessval$ be a positive integer. Prove that there exist polynomials
antipolynil(fractional), antipolyone(fractional), \dots, antipolymax(fractional) (which may depend on $endlessval$) such that
for any integer $fractional$,
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{fractional}{endlessval} \right\rfloor^{endlessval} = antipolynil(fractional) + antipolyone(fractional) \left\lfloor
\frac{fractional}{endlessval} \right\rfloor + \cdots + antipolymax(fractional) \left\lfloor \frac{fractional}{endlessval}
\right\rfloor^{endlessval-1}.
\]
($\lfloor a \rfloor$ means the largest integer $\leq a$.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
For $fractional$ an integer, we have
$\left\lfloor \frac{fractional}{endlessval} \right\rfloor = \frac{fractional-completeval}{endlessval}$ for $completeval$
the unique integer in $\{0,\dots,endlessval-1\}$ congruent to $fractional$ modulo $endlessval$;
hence
\[
\prod_{completeval=0}^{endlessval-1} \left( \left\lfloor \frac{fractional}{endlessval} \right\rfloor - \frac{fractional-completeval}{endlessval}
\right) = 0.
\]
By expanding this out, we obtain
the desired polynomials antipolynil(fractional), \dots, antipolymax(fractional).

\textbf{Remark:} Variants of this solution are possible that
construct the antipolyidx
less explicitly, using Lagrange interpolation or Vandermonde determinants.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp$ be a positive integer. Prove that there exist polynomials
$blrqxkmd(hjgrksla), ntzfjvsa(hjgrksla), \dots, gvsztole(hjgrksla)$ (which may depend on $qzxwvtnp$) such that
for any integer $hjgrksla$,
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp} \right\rfloor^{qzxwvtnp} = blrqxkmd(hjgrksla) + ntzfjvsa(hjgrksla) \left\lfloor
\frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp} \right\rfloor + \cdots + gvsztole(hjgrksla) \left\lfloor \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}
\right\rfloor^{qzxwvtnp-1}.
\]
($\lfloor a \rfloor$ means the largest integer $\leq a$.)
garbled_string.solution:
For $hjgrksla$ an integer, we have
$\left\lfloor \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp} \right\rfloor = \frac{hjgrksla-mndptqvr}{qzxwvtnp}$ for $mndptqvr$
the unique integer in $\{0,\dots,qzxwvtnp-1\}$ congruent to $hjgrksla$ modulo $qzxwvtnp$;
hence
\[
\prod_{mndptqvr=0}^{qzxwvtnp-1} \left( \left\lfloor \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp} \right\rfloor - \frac{hjgrksla-mndptqvr}{qzxwvtnp}
\right) = 0.
\]
By expanding this out, we obtain
the desired polynomials $blrqxkmd(hjgrksla), \dots, gvsztole(hjgrksla)$.

\textbf{Remark:} Variants of this solution are possible that
construct the $fxbqmrcn$
less explicitly, using Lagrange interpolation or Vandermonde determinants.

kernel_variant.question:
Let m be a fixed positive integer.  For every integer x there is a unique decomposition
   x = mp + r ,    r \in  {1,2, \ldots  , m},
and we set
   a\langle x/m\rangle  := p = \lfloor (x-1)/m\rfloor  .
Prove that there are polynomials
   Q_1(x), Q_2(x), \ldots  , Q_m(x) \in  \mathbb{Q}[x]
(depending only on m) such that for every integer x
   a\langle x/m\rangle ^m = Q_1(x) + Q_2(x)\cdot a\langle x/m\rangle  + \ldots  + Q_m(x)\cdot a\langle x/m\rangle ^{m-1}.

(Warning: in general one cannot arrange for all Q_i to have integer coefficients once m \geq  2.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Fix a positive integer m.

Step 0.  Existence and uniqueness of p and r with r \in  {1,\ldots ,m}
-------------------------------------------------------------
Given x \in  \mathbb{Z}, perform ordinary division by m to obtain unique q,s with
   x = mq + s , 0 \leq  s \leq  m-1.
* If s \neq  0 set p = q and r = s.  Then r \in  {1,\ldots ,m-1} \subset  {1,\ldots ,m}.
* If s = 0 set p = q - 1 and r = m.  Then r \in  {1,\ldots ,m} and
   x = m(q-1) + m = mq = x.
Thus there is a unique pair (p,r) with r in {1,\ldots ,m}.  Moreover
   p = \lfloor (x-1)/m\rfloor ,  
so we may write p = a\langle x/m\rangle .

Step 1.  A vanishing auxiliary polynomial
-----------------------------------------
For an indeterminate t define
   F(x,t) := \prod _{s=1}^{m} ( t - (x-s)/m )
           = t^m + a_{m-1}(x)t^{m-1} + \ldots  + a_0(x),
where each coefficient a_k(x) is the k-th elementary symmetric polynomial
in the m rational numbers (x-1)/m,\ldots ,(x-m)/m; hence a_k(x) \in  \mathbb{Q}[x].

Step 2.  Evaluation at t = a\langle x/m\rangle 
--------------------------------
Write x = mp + r with r \in  {1,\ldots ,m}.  For the factor indexed by s = r we
have (x-s)/m = p, so that t - (x-s)/m vanishes at t = p while all other
factors are non-zero.  Consequently
   F(x, a\langle x/m\rangle ) = 0    for every integer x.

Step 3.  Solving for a\langle x/m\rangle ^m
------------------------------
Since F(x,t) is monic in t, the relation F(x,p) = 0 yields
   p^m + a_{m-1}(x) p^{m-1} + \ldots  + a_0(x) = 0,
hence
   p^m = -a_{m-1}(x) p^{m-1} - \ldots  - a_0(x).

Step 4.  Defining the required polynomials Q_i
----------------------------------------------
For i = 1,\ldots ,m put
   Q_i(x) := -a_{i-1}(x)   (with the convention a_m(x) = 1).
Each Q_i(x) lies in \mathbb{Q}[x], and for every integer x we have
   a\langle x/m\rangle ^m = Q_1(x) + Q_2(x)\cdot a\langle x/m\rangle  + \ldots  + Q_m(x)\cdot a\langle x/m\rangle ^{m-1},
completing the proof.

Remark on integrality of the coefficients
-----------------------------------------
Multiplying all Q_i by a common integer clears denominators but also
multiplies the right-hand side of the identity, so the equality would no
longer hold.  One checks directly that for m \geq  2 there is no choice of
integer-coefficient polynomials satisfying the identity for all integers
x (the case m = 2 already yields a contradiction for x = 2,3,4).  Thus
rational coefficients are in general unavoidable.

core_steps: Write n = kq + r, so floor(n/k) = (n − r)/k with r a residue mod k
Form the polynomial ∏_{r in complete residue set}( floor(n/k) − (n − r)/k )
Note one factor is zero (r equals actual remainder), hence whole product vanishes for every integer n
Expand the zero product to express floor(n/k)^k as a linear combination of lower powers with coefficients that are polynomials in n
Define those coefficients as P_0(n),…,P_{k−1}(n) to obtain the required identity

slot1.description: Modulus used in the division algorithm (any positive integer symbol)

slot2.description: Chosen complete residue system modulo the modulus

slot3.description: Limits/order of the product index over the residue set

slot4.description: Notation for the coefficient polynomials in the final identity

slot5.description: Symbol for the integer variable being divided
